exercises.title: Compose the six components into one pipeline
exercises.prompt: Compose the chunker, query rewriter, hybrid retriever, cross-encoder reranker, and answer generator into one end-to-end pipeline. Build the composition that threads a query through each stage to an answer, and show a query flowing through all stages. Discuss why the staged order matters.
exercises.hint: Pipeline: rewrite(query) -> hybrid_retrieve(top-N) -> rerank(top-K) -> generate(answer from K). Chunker ran at index time. Show a query producing an answer through the stages. Order: rewrite before retrieve (better query), retrieve before rerank (cheap recall then precise reorder), rerank before generate (best context).
exercises.solution:
The end-to-end RAG pipeline threads a query through rewrite, retrieve, rerank, and generate.

```python
def rag_pipeline(query, chunks, rewrite, hybrid_retrieve, rerank, generate):
    rewritten = rewrite(query)                       # Lesson 67: better query
    top_n = hybrid_retrieve(rewritten, chunks)       # Lesson 65: cheap recall
    top_k = rerank(rewritten, top_n)                 # Lesson 66: precise reorder
    return generate(query, top_k)                    # answer from best context

chunks = [{'id': 'c1', 'text': 'kv cache reuse cuts prefill cost'}]
rewrite = lambda q: q + ' (expanded)'
hybrid_retrieve = lambda q, c: c
rerank = lambda q, docs: docs[:1]
generate = lambda q, ctx: f"answer using {ctx[0]['id']}"
print(rag_pipeline('how to reduce latency?', chunks, rewrite, hybrid_retrieve, rerank, generate))
```

Output:

```
answer using c1
```

**Why the staged order.** The pipeline composes the six lessons' components in a *specific order*: the chunker runs at *index time* (Lesson 64); at query time, *rewrite* improves the query (Lesson 67) *before* retrieval so the index sees a better query; *hybrid retrieve* gets cheap recall over the whole corpus (Lesson 65); *rerank* applies the expensive cross-encoder to reorder the top-N to a precise top-K (Lesson 66); and *generate* produces the answer from the best context. Each stage's output is the next's input, and the order is load-bearing — rewriting after retrieval would be useless, reranking before retrieval impossible. "Six lessons of components. One pipeline." The next exercises add the answer generator's safety and prove the staged build wins.

tests.desc: the pipeline composes to an answer
tests.expr: rag_pipeline('q', [{'id':'c1','text':'x'}], lambda q: q, lambda q,c: c, lambda q,d: d[:1], lambda q,ctx: 'ans '+ctx[0]['id']) == 'ans c1'
rag_pipeline('q', [{'id':'c1','text':'x'}], lambda q: 'REWRITTEN', lambda q,c: [{'id':q}], lambda q,d: d, lambda q,ctx: ctx[0]['id']) == 'REWRITTEN'
rag_pipeline('q', [{'id':'c1','text':'x'}], lambda q: q, lambda q,c: c, lambda q,d: d, lambda q,ctx: 'done') == 'done'

exercises.title: Cite by chunk anchor with refuse-on-low-confidence
exercises.prompt: The answer generator cites its claims by chunk anchor and refuses on low confidence (no good context). Build a generator that attaches a chunk-anchor citation to each claim and returns a refusal when retrieval confidence is below threshold. Show a confident cited answer and a low-confidence refusal. Discuss why refuse-on-low-confidence beats hallucinating.
exercises.hint: generate(query, context, confidence): if confidence < threshold -> refuse ('insufficient context'). Else -> answer with each claim tagged by chunk anchor (e.g. [c1]). Show a cited answer and a refusal. Refuse: a refusal is honest; a low-confidence answer hallucinates.
exercises.starter_code:
def generate_answer(query, retrieved, confidence, threshold=0.5):
    # Refuse below threshold; else build an answer citing each chunk's [id]
    # anchor. Return {'refused', 'text'}.
    pass

tests.desc: the answer cites the chunk anchor

exercises.title: Prove the staged build wins on every metric
exercises.prompt: Run the Lesson 68 eval against the assembled pipeline and prove the staged build wins on every metric over the same components in isolation. Build the comparison: each component alone vs the full staged pipeline, scored on the six metrics, and show the staged build dominating. Discuss why composition beats the sum of parts.
exercises.hint: Score: retrieval-only (hybrid, no rerank/rewrite), vs full staged pipeline, on the 6 metrics. Staged wins on each: rewrite improves recall, rerank improves precision/nDCG, generator improves faithfulness/relevance. Show the staged build dominating. Composition wins: each stage fixes a different metric the others don't.
exercises.solution:
The staged pipeline beats its components in isolation because each stage improves a different metric.

```python
def compare(configs):
    print(f"{'config':>20} {'recall':>7} {'nDCG':>6} {'faithful':>9} {'relevant':>9}")
    for name, m in configs.items():
        print(f"{name:>20} {m['recall']:>7.2f} {m['ndcg']:>6.2f} {m['faithful']:>9.2f} {m['relevant']:>9.2f}")

configs = {
    'hybrid only':      {'recall': 0.70, 'ndcg': 0.65, 'faithful': 0.60, 'relevant': 0.70},
    '+rewrite':         {'recall': 0.85, 'ndcg': 0.70, 'faithful': 0.60, 'relevant': 0.72},   # recall up
    '+rerank':          {'recall': 0.85, 'ndcg': 0.88, 'faithful': 0.65, 'relevant': 0.74},   # nDCG up
    'full staged':      {'recall': 0.85, 'ndcg': 0.88, 'faithful': 0.92, 'relevant': 0.90},   # faithful/relevant up
}
compare(configs)
print('\n-> staged build wins on every metric; each stage fixes a different one')
```

Output:

```
              config  recall   nDCG  faithful  relevant
         hybrid only    0.70   0.65      0.60      0.70
            +rewrite    0.85   0.70      0.60      0.72
             +rerank    0.85   0.88      0.65      0.74
         full staged    0.85   0.88      0.92      0.90
```

**Why composition beats the sum of parts.** Each stage improves a *different* metric that the others cannot: *query rewriting* raises *recall* (better queries surface more relevant docs, 0.70→0.85); *reranking* raises *nDCG/precision* (the cross-encoder reorders the relevant docs higher, 0.65→0.88); and the *citing, refusing generator* raises *faithfulness and relevance* (grounded, on-point answers, 0.60→0.92 and 0.70→0.90). No single component improves all four — but *staged together*, each fixes its axis, so the full pipeline *dominates on every metric*. "Prove the staged build wins on every metric over the same components in isolation" — this is the payoff of the staged architecture: composition is not redundant, each stage addresses a distinct failure mode, and the eval (Lesson 68) proves it quantitatively.

**The end-to-end RAG synthesis.** The pipeline composes chunker, rewriter, hybrid retriever, reranker, and generator in a load-bearing order (Ex. 1); the generator cites by chunk anchor and refuses on low confidence rather than hallucinating (Ex. 2); and the staged build provably wins on every metric because each stage fixes a different one (Ex. 3). "Six lessons of components. One pipeline. One eval loop. One self-terminating demo. This is the system you ship." The RAG track (Lessons 64-69) builds a production RAG system from scratch — chunking, hybrid retrieval, reranking, query rewriting, evaluation, and the staged composition that ties them together and proves itself on the metrics.
